ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): From the provided summary, the paper applies a Pointer Network to argumentation mining and adds concrete engineering changes (fixing decoding timesteps to input length, using a BiLSTM encoder, and a joint loss with weight α). However, a closely related prior work already applied joint pointer architectures to the same task, so these modifications appear to be incremental adaptations rather than a fundamentally new idea. The paper does distinguish its contributions and reports empirical gains, but the technical advances are modest extensions of existing methods rather than surprising innovations to an expert. | B(3): The paper applies a Pointer Network variant (BiLSTM encoder, fixed decoding timesteps equal to number of ACs) and a simple joint loss to the well-studied task of argument component linking and type classification. These architectural tweaks and the joint training weight α are sensible engineering extensions but constitute incremental novelty rather than a fundamentally new modeling paradigm, particularly given closely related prior work (e.g., a 2017 joint pointer architecture for argument mining). The authors do clearly differentiate their specific modifications (encoder choice, timestep constraint, joint softmax for AC type) and demonstrate empirical gains, but an expert would view this as a moderate advance in method adaptation rather than a surprising conceptual contribution.

The paper does distinguish its contributions and reports empirical gains, but the technical advances are modest extensions of existing methods rather than surprising innovations to an expert.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a clearly NLP-relevant task (argumentation mining) and introduces a modified Pointer Network + BiLSTM joint model that achieves state-of-the-art results on two standard corpora, matching the technical expectations of a top venue. The methodology and baselines are appropriate (compares to prior ILP and embedding baselines) and hyperparameters are reported, though limited-data concerns (microtexts) and the tree-structure assumption—plus no mention of released code—are minor practical issues that temper a perfect fit. Overall the topic, audience, and evaluation align well with top NLP/ML conferences, with only small scope/replicability reservations. | B(5): This paper sits squarely within the scope of a top NLP/ML venue: it presents a novel application of neural sequence models (a modified Pointer Network with BiLSTM encoder) to the core NLP task of argumentation mining, and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on established corpora (Stab and Gurevych 2016 essays; Peldszus microtexts). The problem formulation and joint modeling of component classification and link extraction align with community expectations, the baselines compared are appropriate, and the methodological/evaluation details (hyperparameters, datasets, related work) indicate it is well positioned for the venue’s audience of NLP/ML researchers.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an important practical problem (argumentation mining) and introduces a plausible methodological advance—applying a modified Pointer Network with a BiLSTM encoder and a joint loss for component typing and link extraction—which achieves state-of-the-art results on two established corpora. As the first neural link-extraction approach here, it is likely to change how researchers model link prediction (encouraging sequence-to-sequence/pointer approaches and joint training). However, its impact is tempered by assumptions (tree-structured links), small datasets used, and no mention of code release, which limit immediate broad adoption and generality to more complex argument graphs. | B(4): This paper tackles the central task of argumentation mining — extracting links between argument components and classifying component types — with a novel neural Pointer Network architecture and a joint training regime, producing state-of-the-art results on two established corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts). As the first neural approach for link extraction in this area, it is likely to shift how researchers model argument structure (moving away from feature-heavy ILP and pipeline methods) and to be adopted or extended in follow-up work. However, the impact is somewhat constrained by the tree-structure assumption and small annotated data sizes noted by the authors, so while influential, it is not fully transformative across all genres and real-world settings.

As the first neural link-extraction approach here, it is likely to change how researchers model link prediction (encouraging sequence-to-sequence/pointer approaches and joint training). However, its impact is tempered by assumptions (tree-structured links), small datasets used, and no mention of code release, which limit immediate broad adoption and generality to more complex argument graphs.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (modified Pointer Network, joint AC type+link model, state-of-the-art results on two corpora) are stated clearly and early. The methodology section appears to describe the model architecture and training (BiLSTM encoder, decoder alignment, combined loss with α) and hyperparameters are reported, which aids reproducibility. However, the summary omits which evaluation metrics are used and whether figures/tables effectively present results, and code availability is not mentioned—these omissions are minor but reduce clarity for replication. Notation and examples are not flagged as problematic in the summary, so I deduct one point for these remaining presentation gaps. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (first neural approach for link extraction, modified Pointer Network and joint model), and the methodology and datasets are clearly described (BiLSTM encoder, joint loss with α, persuasive essays and microtexts). Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and thorough, and writing quality and related-work coverage are strong. One minor clarity gap from the summary is that evaluation metrics are not specified, which would help readers interpret the reported state-of-the-art claims more directly.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing the modified Pointer Network and the joint loss (with α) outperform baselines on the two corpora, and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary omits details on evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing, and code availability is unclear, which weakens reproducibility. There is also a modeling assumption (partial tree structure) and sensitivity to small data noted but not fully analyzed for robustness. Overall the technical work appears sound with minor methodological gaps that should be addressed. | B(4): The paper's main claims (modified Pointer Network with BiLSTM encoder and a joint loss for type+link prediction) are supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art results on the two target corpora and comparisons to appropriate prior baselines. There are no evident mathematical errors in the architecture description, but no formal proofs are needed here. The experimental methodology is reasonable (uses established baselines and reports hyperparameters), though the summary omits details on evaluation metrics and statistical significance testing; small-data issues and the tree-structure assumption are acknowledged but could affect generality. Overall the technical contribution appears sound with only minor issues in experimental reporting and scope assumptions.

There are no evident mathematical errors in the architecture description, but no formal proofs are needed here. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper provides a plausible neural approach, compares against reasonable prior baselines on two corpora, and reports hyperparameters — sufficient for an initial validation that the joint pointer model improves over earlier systems (e.g., Stab & Gurevych; Peldszus & Stede). However, important analyses are missing or not reported in the summary: evaluation metrics/statistical significance are unspecified, sensitivity to the loss-weight α and other ablations (e.g., encoder/decoder choices, attention variants) are not described, code is not mentioned, and error/failure analysis is limited to noting the tree-structure assumption and data sparsity. Given these gaps the experiments are adequate but not comprehensive enough to fully support the broader claims. | B(4): The paper presents solid empirical evidence: comparisons to established baselines on two corpora and a clear joint-vs.-pointer ablation (showing the joint model improves performance) support the main claims. Related work coverage is thorough and hyperparameters are reported, but crucial details are missing from the summary (evaluation metrics not specified, code not mentioned) and the analysis of limitations/failure modes is limited to a brief note about tree-structure assumptions. Overall the experimental treatment is strong and appropriate, but reproducibility and deeper sensitivity/failure analyses could be expanded.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a plausible neural approach, compares against reasonable prior baselines on two corpora, and reports hyperparameters — sufficient for an initial validation that the joint pointer model improves over earlier systems (e.g., Stab & Gurevych; Peldszus & Stede). However, important analyses are missing or not reported in the summary: evaluation metrics/statistical significance are unspecified, sensitivity to the loss-weight α and other ablations (e.g., encoder/decoder choices, attention variants) are not described, code is not mentioned, and error/failure analysis is limited to noting the tree-structure assumption and data sparsity. Given these gaps the experiments are adequate but not comprehensive enough to fully support the broader claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents solid empirical evidence: comparisons to established baselines on two corpora and a clear joint-vs.-pointer ablation (showing the joint model improves performance) support the main claims. Related work coverage is thorough and hyperparameters are reported, but crucial details are missing from the summary (evaluation metrics not specified, code not mentioned) and the analysis of limitations/failure modes is limited to a brief note about tree-structure assumptions. Overall the experimental treatment is strong and appropriate, but reproducibility and deeper sensitivity/failure analyses could be expanded.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the main prior task-specific baselines (Stab & Gurevych 2016; Peldszus & Stede 2015 variants) on the same two corpora and reports hyperparameters, and it acknowledges limitations (tree assumption, small data). However, the summary does not indicate whether evaluation metrics and exact experimental conditions were fully harmonized or whether any recent neural baselines or alternative sequence models were considered; the claim of being the first neural approach should be more carefully situated with respect to contemporaneous work. Overall the comparisons are good and relevant but leave minor gaps in benchmarking breadth and explicit fairness checks. | B(4): The paper compares against established and relevant baselines (Stab & Gurevych 2016; several Peldszus & Stede 2015 variants) on the same corpora, which is appropriate. However, a very closely related neural pointer-based approach (the 2017 joint pointer architecture) appears highly relevant and is not listed among the baselines in the provided summary, so the comparison set omits an important recent competitor; evaluation-metric parity is unclear from the summary. Overall the comparisons are strong and mostly fair, but the omission of the most directly comparable neural prior prevents a fully comprehensive evaluation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the main prior task-specific baselines (Stab & Gurevych 2016; Peldszus & Stede 2015 variants) on the same two corpora and reports hyperparameters, and it acknowledges limitations (tree assumption, small data). However, the summary does not indicate whether evaluation metrics and exact experimental conditions were fully harmonized or whether any recent neural baselines or alternative sequence models were considered; the claim of being the first neural approach should be more carefully situated with respect to contemporaneous work. Overall the comparisons are good and relevant but leave minor gaps in benchmarking breadth and explicit fairness checks.

However, a very closely related neural pointer-based approach (the 2017 joint pointer architecture) appears highly relevant and is not listed among the baselines in the provided summary, so the comparison set omits an important recent competitor; evaluation-metric parity is unclear from the summary. main_claims: This paper presents the first neural network-based approach to argumentation mining focusing on link extraction between argument components and component type classification.
A modified Pointer Network architecture (with a BiLSTM encoder) is proposed to extract links between argument components by producing a distribution over input indices.
A joint model that simultaneously predicts argument component types and links (via a combined loss weighted by α) yields substantially better performance than a regular Pointer Network.
The proposed joint model achieves state-of-the-art results on two evaluation corpora (persuasive essays and microtexts).
_summary.methodology: They modify a Pointer Network (sequence-to-sequence with attention) using a Bidirectional LSTM encoder and a decoder aligned to input components, and extend it to a joint model that predicts AC types via a softmax classifier on encoder representations, training with a combined loss weighted by α.
baselines: Averaging embeddings (used by Stab and Gurevych, 2016)
limitations: Models assume a tree structure for linking of argument components (partial assumption).
Small amounts of training data (particularly in the Microtext corpus) necessitate high dropout.

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Focused Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML/NLP engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics and 8+ years building and evaluating sequence models for structured prediction tasks. Experience includes reproducing published systems, creating benchmarks, and deploying models in production.
traits: insists on clear, reproducible experimental setup (code, seeds, hyperparameters, data splits)
looks for strong baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance of reported gains
evaluates model architecture choices and training procedures critically (e.g., loss weighting, decoder alignment)
checks for robustness analyses (e.g., hyperparameter sensitivity, error breakdowns across input lengths and classes)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Analytical and detail-oriented: verifies claims against experimental evidence and requests missing implementation/analysis needed to accept empirical conclusions.

reviewer_b.name: Argumentation Mining Domain Expert
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in argumentation mining and discourse analysis with extensive publication history on argument component identification and relation extraction across genres (essays, social media, microtexts).
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work in argumentation mining and related discourse/linking methods
values clear articulation of task assumptions, limitations, and applicability across genres
notices dataset selection, annotation schemas, and whether evaluation aligns with real-world needs
expects discussion of potential impacts, failure modes, and comparisons to linguistically motivated baselines
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Contextual and impact-driven: situates technical contributions within the literature and evaluates their practical significance and scope.
